title: Surface Properties of Networks Containing Fluorinated Acrylic Monomers
abstract:
Polymers for Advanced Technologies 
Volume 7, pp. 403408 
Surface Properties of Networks 
Containing Fluorinated Acrylic 
Monomers 
Roberta Bong iovanni: N i no Pollici no: 
Gi useppe Gouel i no,' Gi ulio Ma I ucel I i,' 
Aldo Priola' and Bruno Ameduri3 
1 
' Dipartimento di Scienza dei Materiali e Ingegneria Chimica, Politecnico di Torino, C. so Duca degli Abruzzi 24, 10129 
Torino, Italy 
Istituto Chimico Facolta' di Ingegneria Universita' di Catania, V. le Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy 
Ura D11930-CNRS Ecole Nationale Superieure de Chimie, Rue Ecole Normale 8, 34053 Montpellier, France 
ABSTRACT 
The surface properties of acylic films, obtained through 
UV curing of 
bisphenol-A-dihydroxyethyl diacylate 
(BHEDA) mixed with fluorinated acylic monomers, were 
investigated. Three fluorinated acrylates were selected in 
order to vay the film composition, namely: 2-(n- 
perfluorooctylsulphonamido-N-buty1)ethyl acylate, 2-(n- 
perfluorooctylsulphonamido-N-ethy1)ethyl acrylate and 2- 
(n-perfluorooctyUethy1 acylate. They were used at 
concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 1 % w/w. 
The bulk of the films obtained after UV curing was not 
influenced by the additive as far as the thermal and 
mechanical properties are concerned. In contrast, the 
surface properties, examined by measuring the advancing 
and the receding contact angle with different solvents and 
evaluating the surface tension, were deeply modified 
notwithstanding the low concentration of the additive. 
The change was found to be dependent on the type of 
monomer and its concentration. Moreover, a clear influence 
of the substrate in contact with the polymeric film was 
pointed out. The following substrates were examined: glass, 
high-density polyethylene and polytetrafluoroethylene; it 
was shown that the substrate polarity strongly influences 
the surface properties of the films. X-ray photoelectron 
spectroscopy analyses allowed the obtaining of the surface 
composition of the films prepared in the different conditions 
and could provide a clear interpretation of the previous 
results. 
KEYWORDS: fluorinated monomer; acrylic coatings; UV 
curing; surface tension 
INTRODUCTION 
In a previous work 111, it was shown that the surface 
properties of acrylic films obtained through the 
ultraviolet (IN)-curing technique can be modified 
by the addition of long-chain acrylic monomers. The 
presence of these reactive additives can enhance the 
hydrophobic characteristics of the final network, 
depending on the length of the apolar hydrocarbon 
moiety and its concentration. The distribution of the 
additive in the film coated onto a glass slide was 
found to be asymmetric: while the surface in contact 
with the substrate maintained the properties of the 
pure resin, the opposite surface was enriched with the 
hydrophobic monomer. On the basis of those results, 
it becomes possible to design the surface modification, 
by varying the composition of the curable mixture 
and the curing conditions. This is an attractive 
possibility, since many technological applications of 
UV-cured films require well-designed surface proper- 
ties, i.e. wetting, adhesion and coating characteristics. 
Pursuing this research, we considered systems 
modified by the addition of fluorinated monomers. 
The peculiar surface properties of the fluorinated 
products (low surface tension, low wettability, low 
friction) are well known [2, 31. Moreover, their high 
thermal stability, exceptional weathering resistance, 
low moisture absorption and special optical and 
dielectrical properties make the fluorinated mono- 
mers very attractive for the synthesis and modifica- 
tion of high-performance coatings [4, 51. 
In this paper we investigate the use of three 
fluorinated monomers as additives for the modifica-

EXPERIMENTAL 
Materials 
A typical W-curable product (Ebecryl150) was used, 
kindly supplied by UBC, Belgium. Its chemical name 
is bisphenol-A-dihydroxyethyldiacrylate (BHEDA) 
and its formula is 
A series of blends was prepared by adding three 
acrylates, commercially available: 2-(n-perfluorooctyl- 
sulphoonamido-N-alky1)ethyl acrylate, where the 
alkyl chain is either an ethyl or a butyl group were 
kindly supplied by 3M and will be identified as F1 
and F2 respectively. 2-(n-perfluorooctyl)ethyl acrylate 
(F3) was given by Atochem. The structures of the 
three monomers are given below: 
CH2CH2CH2CH3 
F1 
I 
C8F17S02NCH2CH20COCH = CH2 
CH2CH3 
Their concentrations varied from 0.01% to 1% w/w. 
2,2-dimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (Ciba-Geigy) 
was added as a photoinitiator (4% w/w). The liquids 
used for the contact angle measurements were H20 
and CH212. Methylene iodide (99.9%, Fluka) was used 
as received after verifying its purity by means of 
surface tension measurements. Water was carefully 
deionized and doubly distilled by an all-Pyrex 
apparatus. 
Film Formation and characterisation 
The films were obtained by coating the resin on a 
solid substrate with a calibrated wire-wound appli- 
cator to obtain a thickness of about 100 pm. The 
thickness was measured after the curing reaction by 
means of a Minitest 3000 (Elektro-Physik, Koln) 
equipped with a probe for nonmagnetic substrates. 
The substrates employed were glass, high-density 
polyethylene (HDPE) and polytetrafluoroethylene 
(PTFE); they were chosen for their different polarity 
and their different surface properties, as shown in 
Table 1. 
The films were cured by UV irradiation in an inert 
atmosphere, according to the procedure reported 
elsewhere 171. The light intensity of the mercury 
lamp was 12 mW/cm2, measured at the film surface. 
The experimental conditions ensured the complete 
crosslinking of the polymeric films and complete 
insolubility in water and other solvents employed for 
the contact angle measurements. 
Homopolymerization experiments were carried 
out in the same conditions on the pure fluorinated 
monomers: the obtained homopolymers were com- 
pletely insoluble in hydrocarbon and chlorinated 
solvents. The copolymerization of 
acrylic or 
methacrylic monomers, both hydrogenated and 
fluorinated, has been reported in the literature [8, 91. 
In particular, the synthesis of acrylamide copolymers 
with the monomers F1, F2 and F3 was performed 181. 
The copolymerization of acrylates and methacrylates 
containing hydrogenated and fluorinated pendant 
groups was investigated 191 and true copolymers 
with substantial random distribution of comonomers 
were obtained. 
In order to check that the fluorinated monomers 
were completely polymerized, gas chromatography 
(GC) analyses on the unreacted monomer were 
performed. The UV-cured films were subjected to a 
24 hr extraction with n-hexane, then the liquid phase 
was analyzed with a Varian GC instrument, equipped 
with a MegaBore DBWax column (190°C) and a 
thermo conductivity detector (TCD) detector (250°C). 
In the case of F3 the residual amount of monomer was 
found to be about 1/100 of the quantity added to the 
resin. In the case of F1 and F2 this analysis could not 
be performed owing to their very low volatility. The 
bulk properties of the films were reported elsewhere 
and did not change in the presence of the different 
additives [lo]. The glass transition temperature (Tg) 
was found to be 30°C by differential scanning 
calorimetry (DSC) measurement and 40°C by dy- 
namic mechanical measurements (maximum of the 
tan S curve). 
Methods 
The films were carefully peeled off after the curing 
reaction and contact angle measurements were 
performed on them on both sides with the advancing 
and receding drop technique [ll] by means of a Kruss 
G1 goniometer. Wherever the film had the same 
surface properties on both sides, the measurements 
were repeated by the Wilhelmy technique using a

properties of acrylic films obtained through the
ultraviolet (IN)-curing technique can be modified
by the addition of long-chain acrylic monomers. The
presence of these reactive additives can enhance the

depending on the length of the apolar hydrocarbon
moiety and its concentration. The distribution of the
additive in the film coated onto a glass slide was
found to be asymmetric: while the surface in contact
with the substrate maintained the properties of the
pure resin, the opposite surface was enriched with the
hydrophobic monomer. On the basis of those results,
it becomes possible to design the surface modification,
by varying the composition of the curable mixture
and the curing conditions. This is an attractive
possibility, since many technological applications of

ties, i.e. wetting, adhesion and coating characteristics.

modified by the addition of fluorinated monomers.

dielectrical properties make the fluorinated mono-
mers very attractive for the synthesis and modifica-
tion of high-performance coatings [4, 51.

fluorinated monomers as additives for the modifica-

Surface Properties of the UV-cured Films Coated on
Glass: Effect of the Additive and its Concentration
W curing of the pure epoxy acrylic resin BHEDA,
coated on a glass slide, gives rise to a transparent film
whose contact angle with water is 70" on the substrate
side (glass side) and 75" on the air side.
When a fluorinated monomer is added to the W-
curable product and the mixture is cured in the same
conditions, the surface properties of the photopoly-
merized film change, as shown in Fig. 1 where the
contact angle values, obtained with water, on the two
sides of the film are plotted as a function of the
additive concentration. The data clearly show that the
monomer F1 is effective only on the air side of the
films, while the side in contact with the substrate
keeps the same properties as the pure resin. The
contact angle measured on the air side depends on the
additive concentration and an asymptotic behaviour
is revealed. The addition of less than 0.5% w/w
monomer strongly increases the film hydrophobicity;
above this concentration, there is a distinctive plateau,
where the contact angle has a very high value, greater
Monomer concentration, % w / w
TABLE 2. Effect of the Fluorinated Monomers on Film
than loo", typical of a complete hydrophobic surface
such as PTFE [61. A higher content of the monomer,
up to its maximum solubility in the acrylate resin,
does not cause any further modification of the surface.
The other additives, F2 and F3, showed the same
behaviour as a function of the concentration: the films
were found to be asymmetric and, as far as the air side
was concerned, were made highly hydrophobic. The
plateau values, related to the air side, are reported in
Table 2. No additive modifies the glass side. On the
air side of the cured films, monomers El and F2
determine a similar hydrophobicity value; monomer
F3 is slightly less effective.
A similar behaviour was found when additives
having other structures, either linearly chained
nonfluorinated acrylates [ll or alkoxysilane acrylates
[121 were used. Contact angle values of the order of
magnitude found with the fluorinated compounds
were measured only when additives with a much
longer chain length were employed; moreover a much
higher concentration of the additive was required. As
reported in 111, the acrylic esters, which can be
considered the nonfluorinated homologues of mono-
mer F3, at 1% concentration have no effect on the
contact angle by using the longest hydrocarbon chain
additive, n-octadecylacrylate, the asymptotic value of
98" is obtained at a concentration of about 5%.
By measuring the contact angle with methylene
iodide, the surface tension of the films as a function of
the additive and of its concentration was calculated,
through the harmonic mean approximation [6]. The
values of 7s and yp are plotted in Fig. 2 for the

3. The results of Fig. 2 and Table 3 clearly confirm the

In the limits of the experimental errors, in the
presence of any monomer, at any concentration
investigated, the glass side has the same surface
tension as the pure resin: its value is about 45 mN/m,
which is typical of a medium hydrophilic polymeric
surface. In contrast, the air side is made more
hydrophobic in the presence of the fluorinated
compound and shows a lower surface tension value.
Overcoming a 0.5% concentration of the fluorinated
additive, 7s gets a value similar to that of fluorinated
It is interesting to note that the decrease of the
total surface energy is mainly due to a dramatic
decrease of its polar component, rP. These results are
in agreement with a surface enrichment of the
fluorinated monomer, as confirmed by XPS measure-
Besides the contact angle and the surface tension
results, further information on the film surfaces were
obtained by the contact angle hysteresis, i.e. the
difference between the advancing and the receding
angles. Hysteresis is expected from most materials;
among the polymers it is particularly seen from those
having both hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups. In
fact the main reasons for this phenomenon are related
FIGURE 3. InHuence of monomer F1 on the contact angle
FIGURE 4. Substrate influence on the contact angle of the
different films: substrate side.
to the surface roughness and to the surface hetero-
Looking at the reference film, since complete
smoothness can hardly be achieved in real surfaces,
and the pure resin BHEDA contains groups of
different polarities both factors are likely to con-
tribute to its hysteresis, whose value is 28" and 35" for
the air side and the glass side respectively. The
addition to the resin of the fluorinated monomers F1,
F2, F3 increases the heterogeneity of the material;
indeed an increase in hysteresis is observed. In the
case of UV-cured films containing monomer F1, the
results are plotted as a function of the monomer
concentration in Fig. 3: the hysteresis increase
concerns the air side only, indicating that the
additive concentrates there, while for the glass side
the hysteresis remains practically constant. Looking at
the air side values, one sees an asymptotic behaviour:
the surface heterogeneity increases at first, then keeps
a constant value. Similar behaviour was obtained by

Surface Properties of UV-cured Films: Effect of the
A series of films containing the different fluorinated
additives at a fixed concentration, 1.0%, were
prepared by coating the mixtures on the substrates
listed in Table 1. The contact angles of water on the
surfaces of these films are reported in Figs 4 and 5 for
the substrate sides and the air sides, respectively.
Concerning the film surface in contact with air, the
contact angle is not dependent on the substrate, but
only on the kind of monomer: it is always found more
hydrophobic than the reference film, using both the
apolar substrates, HDPE and PTFE, and the polar one,
i.e. glass. As already discussed for the films coated on
glass, F1 and F2 induce a stronger hydrophobicity and
change the surface properties more deeply than F3.
By examining the results of the contact angle
measurements performed on the side in contact with
the substrate, it can be seen that the films have the
same wettability as the reference pure resin when the
substrate is highly polar, i.e. glass, but the surface
properties are dramatically changed when either
PTFE or HDPE is used as a substrate. In these
conditions the contact angle values are very high
and close to those found on the air side.
Concerning the surface tension of these films, the
results collected in Table 4 are in agreement with the
previous data and show that the air side of all films
and the substrate side of the films prepared on HDPE
and PTFE are modified by the fluorinated monomer
and acquire a lower surface tension. The surface
activity of the monomers is FlzFbF3. In other
words, these values confirm the selective concentra-
TABLE 6. XPS Results for the Sample Containing Monomer F1
Substrate side Air side Substrate side Air side
tion of the monomer on the air side and on the apolar
Table 5 gathers the hysteresis values for the cured
films prepared in different conditions in the presence
of the fluorinated additives. Wherever the monomer
showed its presence, as revealed through the contact
angle data and the surface tension values, the
hysteresis is bigger than the pure resin film.
Consequently when the substrate is glass, only on
the air side there is an increase in hysteresis. As
discussed before, on this surface there is a lower
Using HDPE as a substrate, owing to the presence
of the monomer on both the air side and of the
substrate side of the film, the results of the hysteresis
are very close. In the case of polytetrafluoroethylene
the values are high and asymmetric: the hysteresis is
much bigger on the substrate side. This very high
value can be attributed to the monomer surface
enrichment and to the high surface roughness, which
is typical of the PTFE substrate.
Surface Composition Through XPS Analysis
The previous results, obtained through contact angle
measurements, indicate that the addition of the
fluorinated additives to the pure resin induces, under
some conditions, a strong modification of the surfaces.
Therefore the surface composition was analyzed
through XPS. A complete discussion of the spectra is
reported elsewhere [131; as far as the peaks due to the
Cls, Fls, 01s are concerned, their atomic ratios are
reported here in Tables 6 and 7. It can be seen that the
content of the fluorinated monomer on the surface is
higher than expected according to the composition of
TABLE 7. XPS Results for the Sample Containing Monomer F3
Substrate side Air side Substrate side Air side
the original mixture. Moreover the surface concentra-
tion of the additive is a function of the coating
interface. Its value is about 50 times larger at the air
side surface than in the bulk; at the substrate side
surface the content of fluorine depends on the
substrate structure: the concentration is particularly
high when the less polar materials are used, i.e. HDPE
From the O/C values one can obtain some
information on the distribution of the polar groups
of the resin and of the monomer, mainly -COO
groups. The results show a surface concentration
slightly higher than the bulk for all films and a
particular surface enrichment of the most polar
interface, i.e. the glass. Similar results were obtained
in our previous paper [l]: they were attributed in part
to a radical trapping effect and consequent oxidation
of the surface. Therefore the surface composition
depends not only on the resin composition, but also
Wettability, contact angle hysteresis, surface tension
and XPS results show the outstanding properties of
the fluorinated monomers added to W-curable
oligomers. Notwithstanding their low concentration,
they can considerably modify the surface structure
and the surface properties of the films obtained
through the photopolymerization of the mixture in
which they are introduced. The low amounts needed
avoid affecting the bulk properties, as discussed
The surface activity of the fluorinated monomers
is connected to their ability to concentrate at the
surface, as already reported in the literature [141. In
the systems investigated here, when the curable
mixture is coated on the substrate, the fluorinated
monomers selectively enrich the surface by diffusing
through the liquid bulk. When the curing takes place,
the additives react and become chemically bonded to
the network surface. The new surfaces formed are
found to be stable and their properties independent of
time passed since the film formation.
The molecules employed in this work, added in a
very small amount to a typical resin, permitted the
formation of a totally hydrophobic surface very like a
PTFE surface. Among the additives, a surface activity
Considering the molecular weight of the addi-
tives, the order is in contrast to the obvious fact that
diffusion is easier for small molecules. Therefore the
results indicate that the tertiary sulfonamide group
increases the apolar character and the surfactant
properties of the additives F1 and F2 with respect to
F3. Thus the influence of the alkyl group prevails on
the polar contribution of the sulfonamide function-
Several tests performed in this research demon-
strate that the surface modification by the fluorinated
monomers are also dependent on the substrate. When
a polar substrate is used, the monomer is found
enriched only on the air interface; as a consequence of
that, the film is asymmetric, having hydrophobic
characteristics on the air side while the opposite side
is not modified. When the substrate is apolar, the
monomer is present on both interfaces.
These results make it possible to design the
polymer surface on the basis of the resin composition
and on the coating conditions, while maintaining the
bulk properties constant; they further confirm the
great flexibility of the UV-curing technique, making it
suitable for interesting applications.
Thanks are due to 3M and Atochem for supplying the
fluorinated monomers. This work was supported by PF
CNR "Chimica Fine 11" and Italian Murst 40% funds.
